content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA consisting of NNN positive integers.

In one operation, you can replace an element AiA_iAi​ (1≤i≤N)(1\le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) with any positive integer XXX (1≤X≤109)(1\le X \le 10^9)(1≤X≤109).

For each 1≤i≤N1\le i \le N1≤i≤N, find the **minimum** number of operations required to make the number of modes in the array **exactly** equal to iii.

If it is not possible to do so for any iii, print −1-1−1 corresponding to that iii.

Note that modes of the array are elements with the maximum frequency. For example, the array [1,1,2,2,3,4][1,1,2,2,3,4][1,1,2,2,3,4] has 222 modes, which are 111 and 222.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the size of the array.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output NNN spaced integers, where the ithi^{th}ith integer denotes the minimum operations required to make the number of modes in the array exactly equal to iii.


If it is not possible to do so for any iii, print −1-1−1 corresponding to that iii.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Initially, the array [1,2,3,1,2][1, 2, 3, 1, 2][1,2,3,1,2] has two modes (1(1(1 and 2)2)2).

i=1i = 1i=1: Changing the value at index 111 to 555 gives an array [5,2,3,1,2][5,2,3,1,2][5,2,3,1,2]. There is only 111 mode in this array, that is 222.
i=2i = 2i=2: The original array has 222 modes, thus we require 000 operations to have 222 modes in the array.
i=3i = 3i=3: We cannot have 333 modes in the array using any number of operations.
i=4i = 4i=4: We cannot have 444 modes in the array using any number of operations.
i=5i = 5i=5: Changing the values at indices 111 and 222 to 555 and 666 respectively gives the array [5,6,3,1,2][5,6,3,1,2][5,6,3,1,2]. This array has five modes as frequency of all elements is same.

Test case 222: Initially, the array [2,6,6,4,2,1][2,6,6,4,2,1][2,6,6,4,2,1] has two modes (6(6(6 and 2)2)2).

i=1i = 1i=1: Changing the value at index 222 to 777 gives an array [2,7,6,4,2,1][2,7,6,4,2,1][2,7,6,4,2,1]. There is only 111 mode in this array, that is 222.
i=2i = 2i=2: The original array has 222 modes, thus we require 000 operations to have 222 modes in the array.
i=3i = 3i=3: Changing the value at index 666 to 444 gives an array [2,6,6,4,2,4][2,6,6,4,2,4][2,6,6,4,2,4]. There are 333 modes in this array.
i=4i = 4i=4: We cannot have 444 modes in the array using any number of operations.
i=5i = 5i=5: We cannot have 555 modes in the array using any number of operations.
i=6i = 6i=6: Changing the values at indices 111 and 222 to 777 and 888 respectively gives the array [7,8,6,4,2,1][7,8,6,4,2,1][7,8,6,4,2,1]. This array has six modes as frequency of all elements is same.
